title: Project: Terminal Map
summary: Build a small folder map for a field-notes project by checking your location, creating nested folders, and adding files with core Linux terminal commands.

sketchBlocks.title: Start by checking where you are
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When you work in the Linux terminal, your commands happen in a **current folder**. Before creating anything, it helps to confirm your location with `pwd` and see what is already there with `ls`.

- `pwd` shows your current path
- `ls` lists the files and folders in the current location

Here is a simple example:

```bash
pwd
ls
```

Step by step:

1. `pwd` prints the folder you are currently in.
2. `ls` shows what that folder contains.
3. Together, they help you avoid creating files in the wrong place.

If `pwd` shows a lesson workspace folder and `ls` shows only a few starter items, you know you are in the right place to begin your project.

sketchBlocks.title: Build nested folders with mkdir and move with cd
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A folder map becomes useful when you organize related work into smaller folders. In the terminal, `mkdir` creates folders and `cd` moves you into them.

A common pattern is:

```bash
mkdir -p map/regions/north
cd map
ls
```

Line by line:

1. `mkdir -p map/regions/north` creates the full nested path in one command.
2. `cd map` moves into the `map` folder.
3. `ls` lets you confirm that `regions` is inside it.

The `-p` option is helpful because it creates parent folders that do not exist yet. That saves time when you want a deeper structure.

sketchBlocks.title: Add marker files to complete the map
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Folders show structure, but files often show purpose. You can create empty marker files with `touch`.

Example:

```bash
cd map/regions/north
touch landmarks.txt supplies.txt
ls
```

What each line does:

1. `cd map/regions/north` moves into the target folder.
2. `touch landmarks.txt supplies.txt` creates two empty files.
3. `ls` confirms the files are there.

This is a practical way to sketch a project layout before adding real content. In this topic, your final map will use folders and files together so the workspace clearly shows your navigation and creation skills.

quizDraft.title: Project milestone 1: Survey the workspace
quizDraft.prompt: You are setting up a field-notes project in the Linux terminal. First, confirm where you are and what is already in the workspace. Run commands that print your current folder and list the items in it. This matters because a terminal map starts with knowing your starting location before you create anything.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use one command to show the current path and another to list the current folder contents.

help.concept: This task checks orientation in the terminal. `pwd` tells you the current working directory, and `ls` shows the files and folders available there before you begin building a structure.
help.hint_1: Run the command that prints the working directory, then run the command that lists items in the current directory.
help.hint_2: Your command history should include both an inspection of location and an inspection of contents, in the lesson workspace itself.

quizDraft.title: Project milestone 2: Create the map folders
quizDraft.prompt: Now build the folder structure for your field-notes map. Create a top-level folder named `terminal_map`, then inside it create `regions/north` and `regions/south`. After creating the structure, move into `terminal_map` and list its contents so you can verify the map layout. This matters because nested folders are the backbone of an organized workspace.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Use one command that can create nested folders, then move into the top-level folder and inspect it.

help.concept: This task combines folder creation and navigation. `mkdir -p` can create a full nested path at once, and `cd` plus `ls` lets you verify the structure from inside the project folder.
help.hint_1: Create `terminal_map/regions/north` and `terminal_map/regions/south`, then change into `terminal_map` before listing what is inside.
help.hint_2: A single `mkdir -p` command can create both nested region paths if you provide both paths in the same command.

quizDraft.title: Project milestone 3: Add map marker files
quizDraft.prompt: Finish the field-notes map by adding marker files. Starting from the working structure, create `landmarks.txt` inside `terminal_map/regions/north` and `checklist.txt` inside `terminal_map/regions/south`. Then list both region folders so the completed map is visible in the terminal output. This matters because a useful terminal map includes both folders and files that show what each area is for.
quizDraft.template: A file-writing code exercise should also ____ so automated tests can check it.
quizDraft.hint: Move into each region folder or use full relative paths with `touch`, then list each folder to confirm the files.

help.concept: This task extends an existing folder structure with files. `touch` creates empty files, and `ls` helps verify that each file ended up in the correct region folder.
help.hint_1: Use the `terminal_map/regions/north` and `terminal_map/regions/south` paths when creating the files, then inspect each folder separately.
help.hint_2: After creating the files, run `ls` on both region folders so the terminal output shows `landmarks.txt` in north and `checklist.txt` in south.
